coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as an emerging class of nascent carbon-based materials, demonstrate remarkable promise in fluorescence sensing applications. Those potentials stem from several factors, including their favorable photoluminescence (PL) characteristics, feasibility of surface functionalization, excellent biocompatibility, and low cytotoxicity. This review concentrates on the fundamental optical properties of GQDs, with specific reference to the manipulation of intrinsic characteristics both by heteroatom doping and surface/edge functionalization. These modifications permit the alteration of optical properties, thereby rendering GQDs more versatile for an array of applications. Subsequently, we then delve into the recent applications of GQDs in fluorescence sensing, encompassing both turn-off and turn-on mechanisms. Finally, it presents a systematic assessment of the current state of research on GQDs, along with discussions on challenges and prospects for expanding and improving their applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as an emerging class of nascent carbon-based materials, demonstrate remarkable promise in fluorescence sensing applications. Those potentials stem from several factors, including their favorable photoluminescence (PL) characteristics, feasibility of surface functionalization, excellent biocompatibility, and low cytotoxicity. This review concentrates on the fundamental optical properties of GQDs, with specific reference to the manipulation of intrinsic characteristics both by heteroatom doping and surface/edge functionalization. These modifications permit the alteration of optical properties, thereby rendering GQDs more versatile for an array of applications. Subsequently, we then delve into the recent applications of GQDs in fluorescence sensing, encompassing both turn-off and turn-on mechanisms. Finally, it presents a systematic assessment of the current state of research on GQDs, along with discussions on challenges and prospects for expanding and improving their applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nanomaterials are nanoscale materials that are primarily composed of carbon atoms and exhibit various forms and structures due to strong sp 2 or sp 3 hybrid bonds between carbon atoms [1–5]. Graphene, as a relatively new material of the carbon family, is essentially constructed from an isolated layer of sp 2-bonded carbon atoms, forming a two-dimensional (2D) structure with a honeycomb lattice network [6,7]. Due to distinctive and innovative electronic, thermal, and mechanical properties, it has garnered considerable attention in various fields [8–10]. Nevertheless, photoluminescence (PL) behavior cannot be observed in pristine graphene owing to the absence of an optical bandgap in Dirac cone, thereby constraining its potential for optoelectronic applications [11].

In 2010, Pan's team pioneered the production of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) through hydrothermal method, which resulted in the formation of bright blue PL [12]. Since then, GQDs have garnered significant scientific attention. GQDs, with lateral dimensions smaller than 10 nm, possess a distinct graphene structure regardless of their size, thus preserving the exceptional properties of graphene [13–15]. Furthermore, the quantum confinement effect (QCE) and edge state endow GQDs with distinctive advantages, such as a tunable bandgap, high photostability, and biocompatibility [16–18]. In addition, GQDs possess a range of intriguing PL phenomena that are not observed in pristine graphene. Modifications to the bandgap of GQDs have a considerable impact on their optical properties. The bandgap can be adjusted by altering the dimension or functionalization of GQDs, leading to tunable PL characteristics and enabling them potential candidates for a series of sensing applications [19,20]. In parallel to their optical properties, the electrical properties also contribute to their attractiveness. The high surface area and small dimension of GQDs provide numerous edge sites that facilitate charge transfer and redox reactions [21], thereby offering opportunities for applications of photo/electrocatalysis [22–24], and semiconductor devices [25–27], etc.

While the optical properties of GQDs offer a plethora of potential applications, their practical utility is most evident in sensing [28–30]. The exceptional features of GQDs render them a desirable candidate to develop sensitive, selective, and stable sensors. GQDs can be readily functionalized with a variety of inorganic/organic molecules or biological species because of their π-π conjugation, ensuring excellent water solubility [31,32]. This permits the optical properties to be tuned by chemical surface modulation as well as making them accessible as highly biocompatible and versatile fluorescent probes. GQDs have been employed in the design of sensors for analytes, from small molecules such as glucose to larger biomolecules including DNA and proteins, demonstrating their remarkable potential. Hence, this thesis is primarily concerned with a comprehensive discussion of their optical properties and the factors that influence their performance (Fig. 1 ). Subsequently, a comprehensive account of the latest development in diverse fluorescence (FL) mechanisms (turn-off, turn-on) is then systematically provided. In conclusion, this review proposes the potential future challenges and breakthroughs for GQDs.

The preparation methods of GQDs have been the subject of wide-ranging investigations, resulting in notable advancements. A principal objective in this field is to develop synthetic methodologies that yield GQDs with exceptional crystallinity, homogeneity, and monodispersity, which are critical for their application in optical sensing. It is widely acknowledged that the structure of GQDs is predominantly shaped by the synthesis method, rather than the initial materials employed. Generally, the synthesis methods can be divided into two major categories: top-down and bottom-up.

Top-down approach entails synthesizing GQDs by fragmenting an array of carbon materials, such as carbon black, graphite, and carbon fiber. The inexpensive raw materials and straightforward synthesis process of this approach result in GQDs with abundant functional groups, which promote excellent dispersion in solvents and subsequent functionalization. However, this method frequently results in inconsistent size and shape, as well as the formation of potential defects. This approach can be further classified according to the technique used for precursor cleavage, including solvothermal method [33,34], ultrasonication exfoliation method [35], oxidative cleavage method [36], microwave irradiation method [37], and others [38].

Conventional solvothermal (or hydrothermal) methods that employ graphene oxide (GO) as precursor often require more complex processing. This method generally requires strong oxidation treatment followed by cutting the carbon material into GQDs under high temperature and pressure. Nevertheless, recent developments have facilitated precise regulation of the final product either by meticulous adjustment of the reaction temperature or reactant ratios (Fig. 2 A) [33]. Furthermore, this approach is also scalable to the gram level. In contrast, the ultrasonic exfoliation method offers a more streamlined process, requiring only grinding and sonication in solvents, obviating the need for high-temperature treatments, acidic reagents, or complex apparatus. The initial fragmentation of graphene sheets is achieved through grinding, followed by ultrasonic treatment in N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) and dichlorobenzene (DCB) [35]. This resulting GQDs exhibit a transparent golden-yellow color, which can be isolated through centrifugation (Fig. 2B). Currently, El-Kemary et al. achieved synthesis of gram-scale highly chlorinated GQDs following a two-step preparation route with ultrasonication of dicarboxamide and solvothermal preparation [39]. Oxidative cleavage is the most frequent technique of compositing GQDs, entailing the fragmentation of substantial precursor materials through oxidizing agents such as strong acids (H2SO4, HNO3) or superoxides (KMnO4, H2O2) (Fig. 2C) [36]. While this process facilitates the constitution of oxygen-containing functional groups, it often generates a multitude of by-products, the purification of which is a time-consuming process.

Microwave irradiation represents a highly efficient and straightforward method for preparing GQDs employing high-power microwave irradiation (Fig. 2D). This technique requires only a microwave oven equipped with an atmospheric reflux system and integrates the benefits of acid exfoliation and microwave energy. This approach enables the fragmentation of graphite during the one-pot synthesis process through the promotion of multiple redox reactions, thereby facilitating the attainment of high yields and mass production [37]. The experimental parameters of this method are kept to a minimum, facilitating control over the compact lateral size and thickness distribution of GQDs.

Bottom-up approach entails the synthesis of GQDs from an array of organic small molecules or polymers such as microwave method, solvothermal method, electron-beam irradiation etc. Unlike top-down methods, this approach offers the advantage of incorporating heteroatoms and precise control over GQD properties through manipulation of synthesis parameters. However, this approach is constrained by limitations in terms of yield and a pronounced proclivity for aggregation.

Microwave-assisted method presents the rapid and efficient production of GQDs under mild reaction conditions that promote uniform heating, significantly reducing reaction time. Consequently, the method is well-suited for systematically fabricating heteroatom-doped GQDs, enabling precise modulation over content, composition and doping type [40]. Heteroatom-doped GQDs efficiently were achieved through a solvothermal reaction involving norepinephrine precursors, facilitated by simple solvent variation under microwave irradiation (Fig. 2E) [40].

Similar to the top-down approach, the solvothermal method is predominantly utilized for fabricating heteroatom-doped GQDs in a single step, thus obviating the necessity for additional post-processing (Fig. 2F). By adjusting the synthesis conditions, specifically the reaction temperature and the volume ratio of the reaction solvent, it is achievable to make precise adjustments to the Cl-doped and the degree of defects within the GQDs [41]. Electron-beam irradiation has been widely utilized for graphene synthesis due to its mild, simple, and rapid, as well as its potential for controlled mass production at room temperature (Fig. 2G). Depending on the concentration of hydrazine hydrate, a precise amount of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene was added and subjected to irradiation at varying doses using the GJ-2-II Dynamitron electron accelerator equipped with a titanium window [42]. Moreover, the carbonization and pyrolysis of precursor materials (e.g., hyaluronic acid, citric acid) can also be employed to synthesize GQDs [43]. This method obviates the necessity for strong acids or bases, and exact control over reaction temperature is imperative.

GQDs and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are both carbon-based nanomaterials, with GQDs exhibiting physical and chemical properties akin to graphene. GQDs are graphene sheets with lateral dimensions below 10 nm, whereas CQDs possess a more intricate carbogenic core structure. The properties of CQDs differ from GQDs due to the dominance of edge effects in CQDs. Additionally, GQDs exhibit superior photostability against blinking and photobleaching, as well as lower toxicity compared to semiconductor QDs like CdSe QDs, ZnS QDs, and CdS QDs [44]. Table 1 summarizes the advantages of GQDs, CQDs, and other semiconductor QDs. These differences confer unique values and application potentials to each type of QDs in various scientific and technological fields.

The optical transition in GQDs has the capacity to span the ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) and near-infrared (NIR) regions, exhibiting distinctive absorption peaks associated with specific electron leaps [45]. GQDs demonstrate pronounced optical absorption in the UV region (230–320 nm), originating from the π-π∗ transition of the CC bond. Additionally, one shoulder peak induced by the n-π∗ transition of the CO bond can be observed at 270–400 nm [46,47]. Collectively, the lateral size, edge profile, and functional group can influence the spectral properties of GQDs [48,49].

PL is the most prominent and extensively studied property of GQDs, and various synthetic strategies have proven effective in developing GQDs with diverse characteristics [50]. The primary metric for evaluating optical performance is PL quantum yield (QY). To date, the highest PL QY of GQDs has reached 99.8% [51]. While most GQDs with excitation-independent PL properties are obtained through different strategies and exhibit blue or green PL, which hinders their application to some extent [52].

The excitation-dependent PL demonstrates a red-shift across the whole visible range, acquiring the status of a distinctive and intriguing feature of GQDs [53]. The PL mechanism of GQDs can then be summarized as intrinsic state emission arising from QCE, edge state and surface state. QCE is the most extensively embraced model for explaining PL behavior of quantum dots. The infinite excitonic Bohr diameters exhibited by graphene permit the occurrence of QCE for graphene fragments of any size [54,55]. Accordingly, QCE is observed when their dimensions are smaller as compared to their exciton Bohr radius. Furthermore, the edge and surface state represent a promising explanation for PL emission and other optical attributes (up-conversion photoluminescence, two-photon fluorescence, etc.) [56]. Notably, other dependent types of PL emission, including pH-dependent, solvent-dependent and concentration-dependent, have also been documented [52]. However, the PL behavior of GQDs remains a subject of ongoing debate, with extensive studies dedicated to elucidating its underlying mechanisms.

GQDs have aroused considerable interest because of distinctive optical characteristics, which derive from the QCE resulting from the abundance of sp 2-hybridized carbon atoms in their structures. Their optical properties are influenced by spatial dimensions, defects, and doping. Precisely tailoring these properties to produce GQDs with well-defined characteristics has emerged as a key area of current research. Consequently, this paper presents a review of three strategies for modulating GQD properties: intrinsic properties control, heteroatom doping, and surface/edge functionalization.

GQDs exhibit diverse intrinsic characteristics (size, shape, edge states, etc.), influenced by the synthesis methods employed, which contribute to the variability of their PL [57]. The PL emission wavelengths are determined by the energy discrepancy among the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) with the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), otherwise termed the band gap. In general, the band gap can then be scaled up by varying the dimensions of GQDs; larger GQDs exhibit smaller band gaps and longer emission wavelengths. By systematically adjusting experimental parameters such as reaction time, temperature, and reactant concentration, the dimension of GQDs can be accurately adjusted [58]. Beyond size adjustment, the control of other inherent characteristics, from shape to layers and edge state, represents a subject of ongoing research. Kim et al. elucidated the critical role of shape/edge states and size in determining the PL peaks of GQDs via chemical cutting, attributing the anomalous size-dependent PL behavior to the interplay between GQDs shape and edge states [59]. Additionally, Ding et al. demonstrated the fundamental relationship between the structural characteristics and QY of GQDs precursors, employing a bottom-up synthetic approach in conjunction with machine learning techniques. They identified structural, temperature, and concentration parameters as the primary determinants of QY for the synthesized GQDs. Notably, their study was the first to investigate the symmetry of the precursor as a crucial factor in the formation of GQDs [60], utilizing this property to achieve effective modulation of GQDs with an absolute QY up to 83.33% (Fig. 3 ).

Heteroatom doping represents a prospective tactic for modulating the PL behavior of GQDs. The integration of heteroatoms into the graphene lattice provokes a disruption to its characteristic honeycomb structure, thereby engendering a significant alteration to the electronic properties of GQDs and consequently modulating their band gap [61]. Since the initial report of N-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) in 2012 [62], a variety of heteroatoms as N, S, B, F, Cl, P, K, and others, have been successfully integrated into GQDs. In the periodic table, the atomic size of N (1s22s22p3) closely matches that of C (1s22s22p2) and allows for strong valence bonds to be formed with C. Typically, N-GQDs induce the generation of new energy levels within their electronic structure, which can markedly enhance PL emission and QY [62]. N-GQDs are predominantly prepared via bottom-up methods, whereby N-containing dopants, such as urea, are incorporated into the carbon precursor during solvothermal reactions. This process can form C–N bonds with various configurations (pyridinic N, graphitic N, pyrrolic N) [63]. Cui et al. analyzed the non-radiative recombination of pristine GQDs and N-GQDs bearing variable N-functional groups based on Fermi's golden rule. It was proposed that pyridinic N and pyrrolic N accelerated their non-radiative decay slightly, whereas graphitic N, which exhibited a lower non-radiative decay efficiency, could enhance the PL QY of N-GQDs moderately [64]. However, different N configurations in synthetic N-GQDs should co-exist with each other, and the combined effect of different N configurations should determine the true non-radiative decay. Naumov et al. successfully employed glucosamine-HCl as raw material to fabricate N-GQDs using a microwave-assisted hydrothermal strategy in Fig. 4 A. This process enabled the accurate tuning of dimension and band gap, resulting in GQDs that featured stable emission in both visible and NIR regions with PL QY reaching up to 60% [65]. B (1s22s22p1) is another suitable element for doping GQDs, closely resembling C (1s22s22p2) in the periodic table and having one fewer valence electron. B introspects electron deficiency in GQDs, in contrast to electron-rich N, causing electronic perturbations that modulate the PL characteristics of GQDs [66]. B-doped GQDs (B-GQDs) are typically synthesized from GO or graphite powder as the carbon resource, as well as various boron sources, such as B2O3 [67], Na2B4O7·10H2O [51], and others. Gu et al. prepared B-GQDs from Na2B4O7·10H2O via solvothermal technique, which achieved highly efficient green and red PL emission by tuning the solvent (Fig. 4B). The QYs of green and red FL were high up to 99.8% and 17.1%, upon adjustment in the concentration of B dopant [51].

Apart from N and B dopants, some dopants such as F [46], S [45], P [47], and Cl [48], have been successfully introduced into GQDs. F-doped GQDs (F-GQDs) display a distinctive charge distribution and unique chemical bonding within their structure, conferring novel performance that differentiates them from conventional quantum dots. In a pioneering study, Liu et al. synthesized F-GQDs through photochemical irradiation of graphene oxide quantum dots and XeF2 mixture, achieving a maximal blue-shift of approximately 77 nm [68]. Theoretical calculations indicated that the replacement of hydroxyl and carbonyl groups with F upon fluorination resulted in a shifting in PL emission, thereby enabling the regulation of the bandgap in F-GQDs. Additionally, hydrothermal treatment of carbon precursors, such as fructose, in conjunction with various sulfur-containing precursors like H2SO4, MgSO4, and ZnSO4, offers a prospective approach for generating S-GQDs. Wang's team successfully developed S-doped GQDs (S-GQDs) with tunable properties through hydrothermal process of durian, occurring lattice substitution manner (Fig. 4C). The incorporated S resided within the thiophene structures of the S-GQDs leading to excellent optical stability along with an ultra-high QY (79%) [69].

Remarkably, co-doping provides a broader range of avenues for generating GQDs with distinctive properties, including P–N [6], N–S [70], B–N [71], N–F–S [72], and others [73]. Co-doping can facilitate the synergistic coupling effects from heteroatoms to GQDs, thereby improving the PL characteristics of GQDs. Depending on the N doping mode, P, B and S can also influence the emission of the new absorption peaks and band gap in GQDs. Xia et al. reported N, S-doped GQDs (N, S-GQDs) with a QY of 74.5% employing citric acid and l-cysteine as precursor and dopant, respectively, employing a solvent-free bottom-up method [70]. The study demonstrated that S and N, as outstanding electron donors, participated in the π-conjugation of the GQDs, promoting the production of radiative composites and increasing QY. Khoo et al. fabricated B, N-doped GQDs (B, N-GQDs) by microwave pyrolysis and observed that higher concentrations of B doping resulted in blue-shift properties and optical bandgap changes, which were attributed to emission sinks caused by multiple structural defects and grain boundaries [71]. Overall, heteroatom doping represents an intriguing approach that warrants further investigation.

Surface/edge functionalization represents another efficacious strategy for modulating the optical properties. GQDs possess multiple reactive sites and functional groups (amino groups (-NH2), carboxyl (-COOH), carbonyl (CO) etc.), which facilitate further functionalization with other substances resulting in an enhanced PL. Polyethyleneimine (PEI) and polyethylene glycol (PEG), among others are valuable surface-passivation reagents for GQDs, improving PL and providing essential intermediates for subsequent modifications [74,75]. Blue-shifting and red-shifting of the emission maxima of GQDs can be achieved upon coating with PEI of varying molecular weights through amide bonding [75]. Kim et al. controlled the relative amounts of N and O moieties by pyrolyzing the mixture of graphene oxide and PEI, achieving an exceptional QY of 99% and a prominent Stokes shift of 98 nm [76]. Moreover, Jeon et al. fabricated the surface functionalized GQDs functionalized using PEG, hexadecylamine (HDA), and polyhedral oligomeric silsesquioxane (POSS), which resulted in a significant enhancement of PL intensity by factors of 9.0, 5.6 and 9.5 respectively, compared to bare GQDs [77]. Experimental and theoretical calculations revealed that the steric resistance of the functionalizing agents facilitated the reduction of π-π stacking within adjacent GQDs, leading to quench-resistant PL (Fig. 5 A).

Alkyl and amine-functionalization represent effective surface modification strategies due to their particular protonation mechanism [78]. Alkyl-functionalization has the potential to alter the edge structure of GQDs, protecting their PL-active edge sites [79]. Amine-functionalization process forms a specific cyclic structure on the surface of GQDs, with the ammonium fraction being transferred into the conjugated structure, thereby generating strong PL emission [80]. Tetsuka et al. successfully tuned the HOMO/LUMO energy levels of amine-functionalized GQDs by employing different N-containing groups and adjacent groups [81]. This modification caused a notable red-shift of the UV absorption edges and PL band maximum, accompanied by the shrinking of the HOMO-LUMO gap (Fig. 5B). Kim et al. employed laser ablation in liquid, utilizing aqueous solutions of ammonia, pyridine, and ethylenediamine, to synthesize amine-functionalized GQDs from carbon nano-onions [82]. The selection of these dopants enabled the precise tuning of functional group distribution, thereby controlling the PL emission and lifetimes. Moreover, experimental studies demonstrated that a higher concentration of amine groups favored a red-shifted emission with a concomitant decrease in lifetime, while the incorporation of pyridinic groups induced a blue-shifted in PL emission accompanied by an increase in lifetimes.

To identify GQDs on materials, various characterization techniques, including transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), PL, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and UV-vis, are employed. TEM analyses the particle size distribution and morphological features of GQDs; HRTEM and AFM evaluate the crystalline nature and height of GQDs, respectively; XRD determines the crystalline and amorphous phases of GQDs; Raman spectroscopy identifies carbon matrix defects through the analysis of different defect types. Ding et al. reported significant structural changes in N-GQDs observed via HRTEM, revealing a complete sp 2 carbon skeleton in o-PDA-derived GQDs (o-PDA@GQDs) [60]. N atoms within the lattice were directly visualized using high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM). Variations in I D/I G values from Raman spectra indicated that the sp 3 precursors effectively modulated structural defects in GQDs. Herranz et al. employed XPS to identify minor components at binding energies of 284.9, 285.5, and 286.5 eV, corresponding to carbon atoms bound to heteroatoms in C–O/C–N, C–O–C, and CO configurations, respectively [18]. The choice of characterization techniques depends on material properties, functionalization variations, and application focus. Combining multiple techniques allows for accurate identification and characterization of GQDs on materials. Table 2 summarizes the functionality of each method, offering a comprehensive overview of the physical, chemical, and optical properties of GQDs.

GQDs offer significant advantages over traditional semiconductor quantum dots for high-performance FL sensing. Their ultra-small size, high specific surface area, low toxicity, and superior dispersibility contribute to enhanced performance. Moreover, GQDs possess specific functional groups that can interact with analytes in various ways (electrostatic interactions, electron transfer, π-π conjugation), leading to changes in FL intensity. The extensive surface area of GQDs facilitates facile doping or functionalization, enabling specific recognition or dual emission optical properties. These properties render GQDs optimal probes for analyzing a diverse array of targets (Table 3 ). Notably, most GQDs-based FL sensing platforms rely on the turn-off/turn-on mechanism, and this section provides an overview of recent applications in this area.

Many chemical analyses rely on the phenomenon of FL quenching, which occurs when analytes exhibit a reduction in FL intensity through various mechanisms [83]. The reaction between the fluorophore and the quenching agents can cause static quenching, dynamic quenching, or both. The phenomenon of dynamic quenching arises whenever an excited-state fluorophore encounters quenching agents, resulting in a loss of excited state energy through processes such as charge or energy transfer. However, FL quenching occurs due to phenomena such as photoinduced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), and others, which is predominantly employed in conjunction with turn-on. Specifically, PET involves electron transfer between GQDs and quencher, resulting in a donor-acceptor complex that returns to the ground state without photon emission. FRET occurs between GQDs in the excited state and quenching agents in the ground state when the emission spectrum of GQDs overlaps with the absorption spectrum of quenching agents. Generally, as the concentration of quenching agents increases, the excited-state lifetime of the fluorophore decreases.

Qiu et al. synthesized FL ionic liquid-GQDs (IL-GQDs) utilizing 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate as a surface modifier. These IL-GQDs were served as FL probes for detection of S2 –, which selectively and strongly quenched the FL of IL-GQDs through an anion-exchange process. The FL lifetime of IL-GODs significantly decreased with increasing S2 – concentration, suggesting that electron transfer between IL-GODs and S2 – ion was the underlying mechanism responsible for the FL quenching [84]. Tabish and co-workers utilized ethanol as a solvent to produce GQDs directly using biomass waste for the analysis of Fe3+. The hydroxyl/carboxyl groups present on the surface of GQDs produced stable complexes with Fe3+, triggering a quenching in FL emission as excited electrons were transferred to Fe3+. This unique interaction enabled the sensitive detection of Fe3+ with a remarkably low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.50 mM [85]. In another study, Zhang's group proposed a novel approach for selective resonance-enhanced FL of GQDs by utilizing gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) as plasmas for detecting Cr3+ [86]. Notably, the considerable enhancement of PL in the presence of high-concentration AuNPs was determined to the transfer of massive hot electrons between AuNPs and GQDs, followed by relaxation to the ground state. In contrast, the resonant transfer of electrons from AuNPs to GQDs at lower concentrations resulted in an overall enhancement of PL. Experimental studies have revealed that Cr3+ adsorbed onto the central aromatic rings of GQDs with the charge transformation, causing the FL quenching. The detection of Cr3+ ion was ultimately achieved with a correlation coefficient of 97.36% [86]. Based on a similar mechanism, B-GQDs were dispersed in solvents with varying polarities, and their characteristic FL peaks were utilized to construct FL sensing arrays. The addition of plasticizers further influenced the dispersion of B-GQDs and facilitated electron transfer process between electron-withdrawing functional groups in plasticizers and FL molecules, enabling the multidimensional detection of six plasticizers [66].

Most sensors present only a single FL signal and are vulnerable to interference from impurities, compromising their reliability in practical applications. The dual-emission strategy allows for color variation through proportional changes in FL intensity. Ratiometric FL probes can be internally corrected for external interferences and instrument fluctuations, thereby enhancing sensitivity and accuracy [87,88]. For example, a carbon-based nanohybrid system was designed by comprising violet-emitting glutathione-functionalized carbon dots (GSH-f-rCDs) and green-emitting GQDs. This system utilized the relative decrease of the violet emission compared to green emission for detecting of As(III) [89]. The LOD was 0.50 ppb, attributed to specific interactions between GSH and As(III), which undergo electron transfer or other non-radiative electron/hole recombination processes. Meanwhile, Chen et al. fabricated F, N, P-doping GQDs (T-GQDs) as proportional FL probes for ofloxacin (OFL) and l-isomer levofloxacin (LEV) achieving a low LOD of 46 nM and 67 nM (Fig. 6 ) [90]. The detection mechanism was dependent upon the formation of electrostatic bonds between T-GQDs and OFL/LEV, causing an intermolecular electron transfer effect. This resulted in the transfer of electrons from T-GQDs to OFL/LEV, which led to a decrease at 414 nm and an increase at 498 nm. This approach had been effectively employed to the identification of OFL in daily beverages such as fruit juice and milk [90].

Static quenching involves the interaction of a ground-state fluorophore with a quencher to create non-fluorescent complexes, leading to FL quenching. This process is characterized by a change in the fluorophore's absorption spectrum and has been effectively employed for detecting an array of targets [91–93]. Due to the low concentration of unbound fluorophores in the system, the excited-state lifetime of the fluorophores remains unaffected. Mandal et al. designed GQDs at an oxidizing temperature of 50 °C (GQDs-50) for detecting picric acid (PA) with a LOD down to 1.20 μM. The experiments illustrated that the average fluorophore lifetime of GQDs-50 remained unchanged with PA, indicating that the formation of GQDs-50-PA complexes was responsible for the FL quenching [94]. Hai et al. prepared amino-functionalized GQDs (NH2-GQDs) to detect tannic acid (TA) by leveraging its abundance phenolic hydroxyl group. The formation of NH2-GQDs-TA complexes was determined by a combination of hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions and π-π stacking. These interactions might inhibit the activity of the zigzag sites at the edges of the GQDs, leading a reduction in FL emission [95]. In another work, Guo and co-workers established F, N co-doped GQDs (F, N-GQDs) exhibiting tunable PL emission through the synergistic effects of solvatochromism and concentration engineering. In addition, F, N-GQDs exhibited a responsive FL to tetracycline (TC), with a LOD of 77 nM in water [96]. The hydroxyl and carbonyl groups presented in TC molecules formed strong interactions with the hydrophilic groups of F, N-GQDs. Further analysis of the UV and PL spectra indicated that the quenching phenomenon was associated with the internal filtering effect (IFE).

On this basis, the IFE mechanism is proposed. IFE is a phenomenon whereby the FL intensity is diminished due to the presence of a substance (absorber) within the sample that can absorb either excitation or emission light [97,98]. This phenomenon occurs when the absorption spectrum of the absorber overlaps with either the excitation or emission spectrum of the fluorophore. Luo et al. developed proportional FL probes for the detection of doxycycline (DOX) by attaching FL groups (Rhodamine B, RhB) onto F, N co-doped GQDs (FNGQDs). The sensing mechanism was composed of two primary processes: competitive energy absorption and transfer of emission energy. The excitation spectrum of FNGQDs-RhB exhibited an overlap with DOX absorption spectrum in the range of 285–400 nm, which caused a reduced excitation of FNGQDs-RhB, thereby confirming the IFE [99]. Moreover, the overlap between the emission spectrum of FNGQDs-RhB and the absorption spectrum of DOX within the 350–460 nm range supports the occurrence of FRET (Fig. 7 A). The combination of favorable IFE and FRET resulted in a significantly lower FL emission of FNGQDs-RhB, demonstrating excellent linearity at 0.04–100 μM with a LOD of 40 nM. In addition, Jin's group systematically investigated the FL sensing behaviors and mechanisms of GQDs for various heavy metal ions (HMIs, including Cu2+, Cr6+, Fe3+, Co2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, and Ni2+) under different pH conditions (Fig. 7B) [100]. These studies primarily focused on the synergistic effects of IFE, PET, and metal orbital-controlled chelation-quenched/enhanced fluorescence (CHQF/CHEF) effects. Most HMIs were observed to decrease the FL emission of GQDs at pH 5.8 except for Zn2+/Cd2+, while only Cr6+/Fe3+/Cu2+ exhibited a tendency to quench at pH 2.0. These spectral results indicated that weak IFE of Cr3+/Cu2+ and strong IFE of Cr6+/Fe3+ were necessary for FL quenching, whereas other HMIs had no IFE effect. Therefore, both the PET effect induced by interacting GQDs with Hg2+ and the CHQF/CHEF effect incurred by other HMIs were essential for the FL quenching or enhancing. This research establishes a foundation for the development of a multifunctional HMI contamination monitoring platform [100].

Turn-on PL refers to a phenomenon where a system with initially weak or negligible FL is significantly enhanced under specific conditions [101–104]. As previously stated, the CHEF effect generated from interaction of GQDs with partial HMIs at various pHs was key to FL enhancement. Zn2+ and Cd2+ enhanced the FL of GQDs at pH 5.8, while all HMIs except Fe3+, Cr6+, and Cu2+ enhanced the FL at pH 2.0 [100]. Jarujamrus et al. achieved trace detection of Cd2+ using the CHEE mechanism. N-GQDs were chelated with Cd2+ to produce stable N-GQDs-Cd(II), which inhibited PET process, thereby enhancing the radiative decay pathway (Fig. 8 A). This strategy facilitated the detection of real water and herbal samples [105]. Furthermore, magnetic GQDs (Fe-GQDs) had been applied for the "turn-on" detection of metal ions, exhibiting a LOD of 5.10 ppb for As3+. Notably, the addition of As3+ induced a significant enhancement, suggesting that the intramolecular interactions facilitated the formation of Fe-GQDs aggregates. In the aggregated state, the aggregation-induced enhanced emission (AIEE) effect of Fe-GQDs was activated because of the restriction of intramolecular rotation and the inhibition of the non-radiative decay pathway [106].

Off-on strategy represents an effective approach for the intracellular sensing of small biomolecules, reducing nonspecific adsorption and improving the signal-to-noise ratio [107,108]. Zhang et al. constructed an off-on biosensor for the detection of intracellular ATP based on nanosurface energy transfer (NSET) between gold nanocrosses (Au NCs) and GQDs. The GQDs were assembled with Au NCs via aptamers, emitting stable blue FL at 395 nm excitation and being quenched by Au NCs via NSET [109]. The aptamer specifically recognized the nucleotide in the presence of ATP, enabling the separation of GQDs from Au NCs and the restoration of FL with a LOD of 0.27 mM. Cui et al. developed FL probes for H2O2 detection using core-shell Ag@Au nanoparticles (Ag@AuNPs) and GQDs as acceptor and donor, respectively [110]. Ag@AuNPs and GQDs were bound via single-stranded DNA stacking, and their spectral overlap generated a strong FRET leading to a weakening of FL emission. The Fenton-like reaction between Ag and H2O2 produced •OH, which cleaved DNA to achieve FL recovery. The LOD was 0.49 μM, ultimately enabling the monitoring and imaging of H2O2 in living cells. In another work, phenylboronic acid (PBA) was utilized to modulate aniline-functionalized GQDs (a-GQDs) for glucose detection. PBA and a-GQDs underwent π-π interaction, resulting in an electron transfer that led to FL quenching [111]. The specific interaction between BPA and glucose was then exploited to realize the "off-on" process.

Ratiometric FL probes are designed to mitigate background interferences and enable sensitive trace detection by relying on intensity variations in multiple emission bands [112]. Chatterjee et al. pioneered the use of cavitand cucurbit [7]uril (CB [7]) in affinity-driven supramolecular sensing, demonstrating its potential for highly sensitive detection of spermine (SP) and spermidine (SPD) (Fig. 8B). Hydroxyl-functionalized GQDs (GQDs-OH) with a strong, narrow green FL and a cationic donor-bipotent acceptor dye (pyridinium units, BPBP) with red FL were selected as a donor-acceptor pair for charge transfer. The interaction between CB [7] and the portal of GQDs-OH resulted in the withdraw of BPBP from the surface, restoring the FL of GQDs-OH and eliciting a proportional response [113]. The LODs of SP and SPD were 0.10 ppb and 0.90 ppb, respectively, underscoring their potential for practical application. In another study, the same group developed a FRET-based ratiometric strategy for bisulfite using a donor-dual acceptor red-emitting dipyridinium salt (PPy-Br) and GQDs-OH. The introduction of bisulfite disrupted the π-extended conjugation of PPy-Br by Michael adduct formation, leading to FL quenching at 750 nm and an increase at 535 nm [114]. The sensor exhibited a LOD of 36 nM for bisulfite, demonstrating its potential for real-time monitoring of intracellular SO2 levels (Fig. 8C).

This review examines the optical properties of GQDs and their regulation and discusses the research progress of FL sensing application based on GQDs. The PL characteristic of GQDs can be modified through heteroatom doping and surface/edge functionalization. Heteroatom doping introduces new energy levels that perturb the local electronic environment and change the bandgap of GQDs, while surface/edge functionalization attaches other polymeric substances to GQDs to alter their optical behavior. Moreover, the review also provides a detailed overview of GQDs-based FL turn-on/turn-off sensing. Apart from the applications covered above, GQDs have demonstrated potential for bioimaging and therapeutic diagnostics, energy conversion and storage, and electro/photochemical/chemical catalysis. GQDs are acclimated as FL probes for detecting and monitoring the structure and function of cells, tissues, and organs, because of their extremely low cytotoxicity, high hydrophilicity and chemical inertness [117]. Currently, GQDs have made important advances and applications in bioimaging. For example, Lu et al. summarized the latest applications of GQDs in vitro, especially in cell imaging, targeted imaging and theranostic nanoplatform fabrication, as well as in vivo bioimaging [118]. In addition, heteroatom doping, and surface/edge functionalization give GQDs with inherent catalytic properties and the abundant active sites, enabling them to exhibit great potential in catalysis and energy storage. Liu et al. discussed the impact of the structural and other characteristics of GQDs on electrochemical energy-related systems. They highlighted the engineering of related composites of GQDs and their applications in electrochemistry (supercapacitors, water splitting, etc.) [119]. Ho et al. emphasized green synthesis strategies and the biosafety aspects of GQDs. It summarizes the most representative applications of GQDs in the fields of energy and environmental remediation [120]. Beyond that, zhang et al. reviewed the physicochemical, electronic, magnetic, and biological properties of GQDs, along with the advances in the application of GQDs in bioimaging, biosensing, and therapeutics [121]. Currently, GQDs have brought great benefits in different applications and received extensive attention from researchers. Despite the notable advancements, challenges remain to be addressed.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The synthesis of GQDs faces significant challenges that hinder their practical applications. Current methods often result in low yields, making them economically unviable for large-scale production. Additionally, many synthesis routes require harsh reaction conditions, such as extreme temperatures or toxic reagents, which pose safety and environmental concerns. It is therefore imperative to develop novel synthesis techniques that achieve a uniform size distribution of GQDs, ensuring consistent optical and electronic properties across batches. Approaches such as hydrothermal synthesis, laser ablation, or bottom-up chemical methods should be optimized to enhance yield while maintaining the quality of GQDs. Achieving these goals will facilitate the widespread adoption of GQDs in diverse fields, including optoelectronics, sensors, and biomedical applications.

The optical properties of GQDs are intrinsically linked to their structural features, including the π-π conjugation system, edge structure, and surface functional groups. These characteristics significantly influence their luminescence and photostability. Therefore, precisely regulating these properties through strategies such as heteroatom doping, and surface or edge functionalization is critical. Heteroatom doping can modify the electronic structure of GQDs, enhancing luminescent properties and enabling tailored optical responses for specific applications. Meanwhile, functionalizing the surface with various chemical groups can improve solubility and compatibility in biological environments, thus expanding their use in biolabeling and imaging. Continued research in this area is vital to exploiting the full potential of GQDs for applications in sensing, bioimaging, and energy conversion technologies.

To utilize the full capabilities of GQDs as fluorescence probes, a comprehensive understanding of their PL mechanisms is essential. GQDs should exhibit enhanced detection sensitivity, stability, selectivity, and biocompatibility to be effective in practical applications. Research must focus on elucidating the factors that influence PL behavior, such as the dimension, surface chemistry, and shape of GQDs, as well as the nature of their excitonic states. Addressing issues like photobleaching and non-specific binding in biological systems is crucial to improve the performance of GQDs in bioimaging and sensing applications. By overcoming these challenges, we can develop GQDs that not only perform effectively as FL probes but also offer robust and reliable performance for various applications, from environmental monitoring to medical diagnostics.

It is crucial to explore the integration of GQDs with advanced composites such as metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [122] and covalent organic frameworks (COFs) [123,124]. These materials are known to have tunable porosity and surface area that can significantly improve catalytic activity. Research should focus on synthesizing hybrid structures that combine the advantages of different materials, such as combining conductive polymers with nanoparticles to enhance the electron transport rate. It is also crucial to regulate the electronic and structural characteristics of the catalysts by methods such as doping with transition metals or changing the surface chemistry. This approach allows the creation of active sites that selectively promote the desired reaction, thereby maximizing performance in different reaction environments.

How to boost the GQDs loading onto materials? Boosting the loading of GQDs onto materials requires optimizing the interactions between the GQDs and the host material. Key factors to consider include surface functionalization of the host material, functionalization of GQDs, nanostructure of the host material, covalent bonding strategies, equilibrium loading and stability. Firstly, surface chemical modification of GQDs introduces polar groups or ligands to enable specific interactions (e.g., hydrogen bonding, ionic bonding, or van der Waals forces) with the host material. Secondly, introducing functional groups (e.g., –NH2, –COOH) on the host material's surface provides binding sites for GQDs. Surface area can be increased using etching or roughening techniques, offering additional adsorption sites for GQDs. Thirdly, nanostructuring the host material with porous or high surface area materials (e.g., MOFs, COFs) can enhance GQDs loading. Pores trap and anchor GQDs, thereby increasing the loading capacity. Fourthly, covalent bonding strategies utilize cross-linking agents (e.g., EDC/NHS for amine-carboxylate coupling) to create covalent bonds between GQDs and the host material. In situ synthesis of GQDs directly on the host material ensures strong covalent linkages. Finally, equilibrium loading and stability must be considered, as overloading GQDs can cause agglomeration, compromising homogeneity and performance. Matching the physicochemical properties of GQDs and the host material is crucial to maximize interactions and stability. By integrating these strategies, it is possible to significantly improve the loading of GQDs onto materials, which can enhance their performance in applications like sensing, catalysis, and energy storage.

The incorporation of machine learning techniques represents a transformative approach in functional materials development [125]. By employing predictive modeling, researchers can rapidly identify optimal reaction conditions and PL properties, thus significantly reducing experimental timelines and resource expenditures. Existing literatures demonstrate the potential of machine learning to enhance the PL properties of GQDs by exploring the intricate relationships between their structural properties and performance. Diverse machine learning models, including linear regression, gradient boosting algorithms, bagging regression, XGBoost models, and random forest algorithms, can be employed to analyze extensive experimental datasets. These models effectively identify patterns and correlations that are challenging to detect using traditional methods. As these analyses are not constrained by specific outcomes, they can provide an objective insight into the impact of process parameters during material synthesis. Furthermore, developing databases that include comprehensive materials information will enable more effective training of these algorithms, leading to improved predictive capabilities. This data-driven approach will not only streamline catalyst design but also enhance our understanding of underlying reaction mechanisms.

The complexity of GQDs development necessitates cross-disciplinary collaboration among material scientists, chemists, and engineers [126–128]. This collaborative framework can foster innovation by combining diverse perspectives and expertise to tackle multifaceted challenges. For instance, chemists can work alongside engineers to optimize reactor designs that enhance PL performance while ensuring safety and efficiency. Moreover, integrating synthetic processes with renewable energy systems, such as photovoltaic or wind technologies, can create synergistic solutions that drive more sustainable chemical manufacturing practices. Exploring these interdisciplinary approaches will be vital for overcoming the barriers to implementing advanced catalytic systems in real-world applications.

ce:section.ce:para: We hope that the collaborative efforts of researchers will lead to new discoveries and developments in GQDs, from basic cell biology research to clinical diagnosis of human diseases.
Ru Wu: Writing – original draft. Yue Cao: Conceptualization. Zixuan Chen: Writing – review & editing. Jun-Jie Zhu: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis.
